q: Which Xrm object performs CRUD against Dataverse from client script?
Which Xrm object opens forms, dialogs and navigates the app?
why: Xrm.Navigation handles openForm, openAlertDialog, navigateTo and similar UX navigation.

q: Decide whether each global Xrm statement is true.

rows.s: Xrm.Device can access device capabilities like location or camera.
Xrm.WebApi is only for opening dialogs.

why: Device/Encoding provide device and encoding helpers; WebApi is for data operations, not dialogs.

q: Which Xrm namespace offers utility helpers such as getGlobalContext and lookups?
why: Xrm.Utility exposes helpers like getGlobalContext and lookup dialogs.

q: For simple declarative field logic, which is preferred over client script?
why: Business rules handle simple declarative logic without code; prefer them when sufficient.

q: Logic that must run reliably even when data is created outside the form should be...
o: A business rule on the form only
why: Client script (and form business rules) don't run for non-form data paths; use a plug-in.

q: Decide whether each best-practice statement is true.

rows.s: You should run the Solution Checker to catch issues in customizations.
Define unique function names to avoid collisions across libraries.
Using deprecated/unsupported methods is recommended for brevity.

why: Use Solution Checker, unique names, and avoid deprecated/unsupported methods.

q: Two libraries both define a function named 'onLoad'. What problem can occur?
o: A naming collision where one overwrites the other
why: Global name collisions cause one function to overwrite the other; use unique/namespaced names.

q: Which is a supported way to debug client script?
o: Browser developer tools with breakpoints on the web resource
why: Use the browser dev tools (sources/breakpoints, console) to debug the loaded web resource.

q: Before shipping customizations, running the Solution Checker helps you...
o: Detect performance, reliability and deprecated-API issues
why: Solution Checker statically analyzes for problems including deprecated APIs and best-practice violations.

q: Decide whether each debugging statement is true.

rows.s: console.log output can be inspected in the browser console.
You can set breakpoints in the loaded JavaScript web resource.

why: Browser dev tools show console output and support breakpoints in the web resource.

q: A calculated column already gives the value you need on the form. Should you also script it?
o: No — avoid duplicating logic; use the calculated column
Yes — always add client script too
Only if you write a plug-in as well
why: Don't duplicate logic; a calculated column (or business rule) is simpler than redundant script.

q: You must run extra validation specifically on save. Which event do you handle?
why: Save-time validation belongs in the OnSave handler (which can also prevent the save).

q: In OnSave, how can client script prevent an invalid save?
why: preventDefault() on the save event args cancels the save from client script.

q: Decide whether each advanced-scripting statement is true.

rows.s: Xrm.WebApi calls are asynchronous and return promises.
Coding standards recommend unique, namespaced function names.

why: WebApi is async/promise-based; use namespacing; OnSave can cancel via preventDefault.

q: The exercise's client script is added to which artifact so it runs on the form?
o: A form event handler referencing the JavaScript web resource
why: You register the web resource's function as a form/field event handler.
